title: Customer Directory
story: Welcome to ShopKart. You've just been hired as a data analyst, and your first task is simple: get familiar with the data. Retrieve all columns from the Customers table.
pipelineCallout: Notice the pipeline sidebar? SQL starts at FROM — it needs to know which table to look at before it can show you anything. You write SELECT first, but the database reads FROM first.
hints: Use the SELECT keyword followed by * to get all columns.
Make sure you specify the table using FROM Customers.
SELECT * FROM Customers;

title: Product Catalog
story: The warehouse manager just pinged you: "Can you pull everything we have in the system?" Retrieve all columns from the Products table.
hints: This is just like the previous query, but targeting a different table.
SELECT * FROM Products;

title: Customer Contact List
story: Marketing doesn't need the full customer record — just names. Return only first_name and last_name from Customers. This is your first time choosing specific columns instead of using *.
hints: List the column names after SELECT, separated by a comma.
SELECT first_name, last_name FROM Customers;

title: Employee Overview
story: Management wants a quick staff snapshot. Return first_name, last_name, and department from the Employees table.
hints: You just need to list all three columns after SELECT.
SELECT first_name, last_name, department FROM Employees;

title: Unique Categories
story: The head of merchandising asks: "How many product categories do we actually carry?" Use DISTINCT to retrieve each category only once from the Products table.
hints: Put the DISTINCT keyword immediately after SELECT and before the column name.
SELECT DISTINCT category FROM Products;

title: Unique Departments
story: HR wants a clean list of departments that exist in the company. Use DISTINCT on the Employees table to list every department — no repeats.
hints: Remember, DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from your results.
SELECT DISTINCT department FROM Employees;

title: Friendly Column Names
story: You're building a stakeholder report, and the column names look too technical. Select first_name, last_name, and email from Customers and alias them as first, last, and contact. Use the AS keyword.
hints: Use the format: column_name AS new_name
You need to alias all three columns requested.
SELECT first_name AS first, last_name AS last, email AS contact FROM Customers;

title: Full Name Column
story: The customer-facing team wants a single full_name column — not two separate columns. Concatenate first_name and last_name with a space between them using the || operator.
hints: In SQLite, || glues text together. To add a space, use: column1 || ' ' || column2
Don't forget to alias the resulting column as full_name using the AS keyword.
SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name FROM Customers;

title: Processing Orders
story: The logistics team is triaging today's workload. They need all orders that are still Processing. Filter the Orders table where status equals 'Processing'.
pipelineCallout: Look at the pipeline — WHERE just appeared between FROM and SELECT. The database filters rows BEFORE it picks columns. That's why WHERE can see every column in the table, even ones you didn't SELECT.
hints: Use SELECT * FROM Orders, then add a WHERE clause.
Make sure you put 'Processing' in quotes since it is text.
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE status = 'Processing';

title: Electronics Inventory
story: The electronics buyer wants to audit their section. Find all products in the Electronics category.
hints: Filter the Products table based on the category column.
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE category = 'Electronics';

title: High Value Orders
story: Finance flagged a threshold: any order above ₹500 needs a second review. Find all orders where amount is greater than 500.
hints: Use the > symbol in your WHERE clause.
Since 500 is a number, you don't need quotes around it.
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE amount > 500;

title: Low Stock Alert
story: The stock manager's dashboard just lit up red. Find all products where stock is less than 30 so we can trigger reorders.
hints: Filter the Products table where stock < 30
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE stock < 30;

title: Active Orders
story: Operations wants everything that's still in motion. Find all orders that have NOT been 'Delivered'. Use the <> operator.
hints: The <> operator means "not equal to".
You can also use != which does the exact same thing in SQLite.
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE status <> 'Delivered';

title: Pricey Electronics
story: The sales team wants to build a "Premium Electronics" campaign page. Find all products in the Electronics category with a price greater than 50. You'll need AND to combine both conditions.
hints: Chain your conditions: WHERE condition1 AND condition2
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE category = 'Electronics' AND price > 50;

title: Shipments in Motion
story: The shipping partner wants a status update. Find all orders with status 'Shipped' OR 'Cancelled' — they handle both outbound and return logistics.
hints: Chain your conditions: WHERE condition1 OR condition2
Remember you have to repeat the column name: status = X OR status = Y, not status = X OR Y.
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE status = 'Shipped' OR status = 'Cancelled';

title: Keyboard Products
story: A corporate buyer just called: "Show me everything keyboard-related." Use LIKE to find every product whose name contains 'Key'.
hints: The LIKE operator uses % as a wildcard. To find a word anywhere in a string, use %word%.
Use LIKE '%Key%'
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE name LIKE '%Key%';

title: Bulk Non-Electronics
story: The wholesale team focuses on high-volume, non-tech products. Find all products that are NOT in the Electronics category AND have stock greater than 100.
hints: Use both the <> and AND operators in your WHERE clause.
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE category <> 'Electronics' AND stock > 100;

title: Ghost Emails
story: You're about to send a mass email campaign, and someone warns you: "Not every customer has an email on file." Find all customers whose email is NULL.
pipelineCallout: Try writing WHERE email = NULL first. It returns zero rows. That's not a bug — NULL isn't a value you can compare with =. NULL means "unknown." Use IS NULL instead. This is one of the most important lessons in SQL.
hints: You cannot use email = NULL. You must use IS NULL.
SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE email IS NULL;

title: Verified Contacts
story: Now find the opposite — all customers who DO have an email. Use IS NOT NULL. These are the ones safe to include in the campaign.
hints: Filter using WHERE email IS NOT NULL.
SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE email IS NOT NULL;

title: Newest Orders
story: The CEO's Monday morning question: "What's our latest activity?" Sort all orders by order_date in descending order — newest first.
pipelineCallout: ORDER BY just appeared at the bottom of the pipeline. It runs AFTER SELECT. That's why you CAN use column aliases in ORDER BY — the alias already exists by then.
hints: Add ORDER BY column_name DESC at the end of your query.
SELECT * FROM Orders ORDER BY order_date DESC;

title: Top Expensive Products
story: The premium product page needs content. Show the Top 3 most expensive products — include only name and price. You'll need ORDER BY and LIMIT together.
hints: Sort by price DESC and add LIMIT 3 at the very end.
SELECT name, price FROM Products ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 3;

title: Selected Customers
story: A loyalty promotion targets our earliest customers. Find all orders placed by customer_id 1, 2, or 3. Use the IN operator — it's cleaner than chaining three OR conditions.
hints: Use: WHERE customer_id IN (1, 2, 3)
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE customer_id IN (1, 2, 3);

title: Mid Range Products
story: The "Sweet Spot" collection features products that aren't too cheap, aren't too expensive. Find products with a price between 50 and 500. BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends.
hints: Use: WHERE price BETWEEN 50 AND 500
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE price BETWEEN 50 AND 500;

title: Preview Orders
story: Quick sanity check before a meeting: pull just the 5 most recent orders. Combine ORDER BY with LIMIT.
hints: Sort by order_date descending, then take the top 5.
SELECT * FROM Orders ORDER BY order_date DESC LIMIT 5;

title: Department Roster
story: HR wants a clean org chart export. Sort employees alphabetically by department (A→Z), then within each department by salary highest first (DESC). This is your first multi-column ORDER BY.
hints: Separate multiple sorting columns with a comma: ORDER BY col1 ASC, col2 DESC
SELECT * FROM Employees ORDER BY department ASC, salary DESC;

title: Excluding Early Customers
story: For a "new customer" campaign, we need to exclude the original three. Return all orders where customer_id is NOT IN (1, 2, 3).
hints: Similar to the IN operator, just put NOT in front of it.
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE customer_id NOT IN (1, 2, 3);

title: Page 2 of Products
story: Your product listing shows 3 items per page. The user clicked "Next." Retrieve the second page — that's products 4 through 6, ordered by id. Use LIMIT 3 OFFSET 3.
pipelineCallout: LIMIT runs dead last in the pipeline. That means the database processes ALL rows through every step, THEN cuts the window. It's not a performance shortcut — it's a display control.
hints: OFFSET skips the first N rows before returning the remaining ones.
SELECT * FROM Products ORDER BY id LIMIT 3 OFFSET 3;

title: Total Orders
story: The board meeting is tomorrow. First question they'll ask: "How many total orders have we received?" Use COUNT(*).
pipelineCallout: Two new steps just appeared in your pipeline: GROUP BY and HAVING. They sit between WHERE and SELECT. This is the key to understanding why HAVING exists — it filters AFTER grouping.
hints: Just select COUNT(*) from the table.
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Orders;

title: Total Revenue
story: Next board question: "What's our total revenue?" Calculate the SUM of all order amounts.
hints: Pass the amount column to the SUM function.
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM Orders;

title: Average Product Price
story: The pricing team is benchmarking: "What's the average price across our entire catalog?" Use AVG.
hints: Pass the price column to the AVG function.
SELECT AVG(price) FROM Products;

title: Price Extremes
story: One slide, two numbers: what's the cheapest and most expensive product? Return both using MIN and MAX in a single query.
hints: You can use multiple aggregate functions in the same SELECT statement.
SELECT MIN(price), MAX(price) FROM Products;

title: Orders Per Customer
story: Customer success wants to see engagement levels. Count how many orders each customer has placed. Show customer_id and the count. This is your first GROUP BY.
hints: When using GROUP BY, whatever you group by should also be in your SELECT list.
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id;

title: Revenue by Product
story: Which products generate the most money? Show each product_id and its total revenue (SUM(amount)), sorted highest first. GROUP BY + SUM + ORDER BY in one query.
hints: Remember the pipeline order. GROUP BY comes before ORDER BY.
SELECT product_id, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue FROM Orders GROUP BY product_id ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;

title: Unique Products Sold
story: "How many distinct products have actually been ordered?" A product ordered five times should count once. Use COUNT(DISTINCT product_id).
hints: Use COUNT(DISTINCT column_name) to only count unique values.
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) FROM Orders;

title: High Value Customers
story: Find customers who have placed more than 1 order. You can't use WHERE here — you need to filter on the result of COUNT, which means filtering a group, not a row. That's what HAVING is for.
pipelineCallout: This is the moment the pipeline pays for itself. Look at the sidebar: WHERE is at Step 2 (filters rows). HAVING is at Step 4 (filters groups). They're NOT interchangeable.
hints: HAVING comes right after your GROUP BY clause.
SELECT customer_id FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

title: The NULL Trap
story: Here's a sneaky one. Run two queries on the Customers table at once by returning two columns. The first column is COUNT(*). The second column is COUNT(email). Look at the result. Do they return the same number? If not — why?
hints: Just select both COUNT aggregates as separate columns.
Because Alex Patel has a NULL email, COUNT(email) ignores that row, but COUNT(*) counts it.
SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(email) FROM Customers;

title: Pipeline Check: Aggregates
story: This is not a coding problem. You'll see a query and arrange its execution steps in the correct order. Drag the steps into the order the database actually runs them.
query:
SELECT category, AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM Products
WHERE stock > 0
GROUP BY category
HAVING AVG(price) > 100
ORDER BY avg_price DESC
LIMIT 3
debrief: WHERE filters rows (stock > 0) before grouping. HAVING filters groups (avg_price > 100) after grouping. ORDER BY uses the alias avg_price — it works because SELECT already created it.

title: Customer Orders
story: So far you've only queried one table at a time. Real analysis connects tables. JOIN the Customers and Orders tables to show each order's id alongside the customer's first_name.
pipelineCallout: Look at the pipeline — FROM just became "FROM / JOIN". The JOIN happens as part of building the source table. By the time WHERE runs, the two tables are already merged.
hints: Use the ON keyword to specify how the tables link: Orders.customer_id = Customers.id
SELECT Orders.id, Customers.first_name FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON Orders.customer_id = Customers.id;

title: Order Product Names
story: Which product was in each order? JOIN Orders and Products to show order id and product name.
hints: Link them using Orders.product_id and Products.id.
SELECT Orders.id, Products.name FROM Orders JOIN Products ON Orders.product_id = Products.id;

title: Order Details Dashboard
story: The operations dashboard needs the full picture. Show first_name (from Customers), product name (from Products), and order amount — all in one query. This requires joining three tables: Customers → Orders → Products.
hints: You can chain JOINs: FROM A JOIN B ON ... JOIN C ON ...
SELECT Customers.first_name, Products.name, Orders.amount FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON Orders.customer_id = Customers.id JOIN Products ON Orders.product_id = Products.id;

title: Customer Purchase History
story: Build a clean purchase history: show first_name and product name for every order. Similar to the dashboard, but focused on the customer-to-product relationship.
hints: Join Orders, Customers, and Products just like the previous query, but select different columns.
SELECT Customers.first_name, Products.name FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON Orders.customer_id = Customers.id JOIN Products ON Orders.product_id = Products.id;

title: Potential Customers
story: This is the most important JOIN problem in the entire curriculum. Some customers registered but never bought anything. Find their first_name. Use a LEFT JOIN between Customers and Orders, then filter for rows where the order side is NULL.
hints: LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table. Then use WHERE Orders.id IS NULL to find the orphans.
SELECT Customers.first_name FROM Customers LEFT JOIN Orders ON Customers.id = Orders.customer_id WHERE Orders.id IS NULL;

title: Delivered Order Report
story: Finance needs a report. Show all Delivered orders with the customer's first_name, last_name, amount, and order_date. JOIN Orders and Customers, filter with WHERE, sort by date.
hints: Start with your JOIN, then add a WHERE clause, and finally ORDER BY.

SELECT Customers.first_name, Customers.last_name, Orders.amount, Orders.order_date FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON Orders.customer_id = Customers.id WHERE Orders.status = 'Delivered' ORDER BY Orders.order_date;

title: Revenue by Category
story: Which product category drives the most revenue? JOIN Orders and Products, then GROUP BY category. Show the order count (as num_orders) and total revenue per category (as category_revenue), sorted by revenue descending.
hints: Combine joining tables with aggregate functions and grouping.

SELECT Products.category, COUNT(Orders.id) AS num_orders, SUM(Orders.amount) AS category_revenue FROM Orders JOIN Products ON Orders.product_id = Products.id GROUP BY Products.category ORDER BY category_revenue DESC;

title: Premium Deliveries
story: The full three-table JOIN under pressure. Show all Delivered orders with customer name (first_name) and product name, returning `first_name`, `name` (from products), and `amount`. Sort by amount descending, then by order id ascending as a tiebreaker.
hints: Three tables joined together, plus WHERE, plus multi-column ORDER BY.

SELECT Customers.first_name, Products.name, Orders.amount FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON Orders.customer_id = Customers.id JOIN Products ON Orders.product_id = Products.id WHERE Orders.status = 'Delivered' ORDER BY Orders.amount DESC, Orders.id ASC;

title: Department Colleagues
story: Here's something new: joining a table to itself. Find all pairs of employees who work in the same department. You'll alias the Employees table twice (e.g., e1 and e2) and join them on department. Make sure you don't pair someone with themselves (e.g. e1.id < e2.id). Show first_name as employee1, first_name as employee2, and department.
pipelineCallout: A self-join is still just a JOIN — the pipeline doesn't change. You're creating two copies of the same table and matching them.
hints: FROM Employees e1 JOIN Employees e2 ON e1.department = e2.department AND e1.id < e2.id
SELECT e1.first_name AS employee1, e2.first_name AS employee2, e1.department FROM Employees e1 JOIN Employees e2 ON e1.department = e2.department AND e1.id < e2.id;

title: Pipeline Check: JOIN + Agg
story: Arrange the execution order for this query. You'll discover a subtle bug in this query — and learn why it matters in the debrief.
query:
SELECT c.first_name, COUNT(o.id) AS order_count
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.status <> 'Cancelled'
GROUP BY c.first_name
HAVING COUNT(o.id) >= 2
ORDER BY order_count DESC
scrambled: FROM Customers c LEFT JOIN Orders o
correct: FROM Customers c LEFT JOIN Orders o
debrief: JOIN is part of FROM — it builds one combined table first. Here is the bug: WHERE on the LEFT JOIN result filters OUT the NULL rows LEFT JOIN created for customers with no orders.

title: Default Email
story: Marketing needs a complete contact list to export. Some customers lack an email. Provide first_name, and use COALESCE on email to return "no_email@placeholder.com" if it's NULL.
pipelineCallout: NULL affects almost every step of the pipeline. In SELECT, adding a number to NULL gives NULL. In WHERE, testing NULL = NULL is FALSE. In ORDER BY, NULLs typically float to the top or bottom depending on ASC/DESC.
hints: COALESCE(column_name, fallback_value)
SELECT first_name, COALESCE(email, 'no_email@placeholder.com') AS email FROM Customers;

title: Safe Stock Count
story: A new product has a NULL stock value instead of 0 because it's on backorder. Display product name and use COALESCE to show 0 whenever stock is NULL.
hints: Wrap the stock column: COALESCE(stock, 0)
SELECT name, COALESCE(stock, 0) AS stock FROM Products;

title: NULL Arithmetic
story: Attempt to add 10 to every product's stock (e.g., stock + 10). See what happens to the product with NULL stock when math gets involved.
hints: Just select name and stock + 10 AS boosted_stock
NULL plus anything is always NULL. Arithmetic propagates NULLs.
SELECT name, stock + 10 AS boosted_stock FROM Products;

title: Full Contact Card
story: Build a contact card format like "Name <email>". Combine first_name, a space, last_name, and their email inside angle brackets. Handle NULL emails gracefully so the string doesn't become completely NULL.
hints: You'll need both the string concatenation operator (||) and COALESCE.
SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name || ' <' || COALESCE(email, 'no email') || '>' AS contact_card FROM Customers;

title: Complete Records Only
story: The data engineering team wants a clean extract of products for machine learning. Only include robust rows: find all products where BOTH price and stock are NOT NULL.
hints: Use the AND operator to chain two IS NOT NULL checks in your WHERE clause.
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE price IS NOT NULL AND stock IS NOT NULL;

title: Add a New Customer
story: A VIP just signed up in person. Insert Rahul Sharma into the Customers table. Use ID 11, email "r.sharma@example.com", and registration_date "2023-10-25".
pipelineCallout: DML operations have their own mini-pipeline logic. INSERT directly adds a row to the table. It bypasses SELECT entirely.
hints: Use the INSERT INTO ... VALUES format.
Make sure string values are properly quoted.
INSERT INTO Customers (id, first_name, last_name, email, registration_date) VALUES (11, 'Rahul', 'Sharma', 'r.sharma@example.com', '2023-10-25');

title: New Product Arrival
story: The warehouse just logged a new shipment. Insert a Gaming Headset into Products with ID 11, Category "Electronics", Price 99.99, and Stock 40.
hints: Follow the same INSERT pattern as the previous problem.
Make sure numeric values do not have quotes.
INSERT INTO Products (id, name, category, price, stock) VALUES (11, 'Gaming Headset', 'Electronics', 99.99, 40);

title: Restock Inventory
story: We just restocked laptops. UPDATE the Products table to set the stock of the Laptop to 50.
hints: Use UPDATE table SET col = value WHERE condition.
Filter by name = 'Laptop'.
UPDATE Products SET stock = 50 WHERE name = 'Laptop';

title: Correct Order Amount
story: Customer support caught a billing error on order 102. UPDATE Orders to set its amount to 40.00.
hints: Identify the specific order using WHERE id = 102.
UPDATE Orders SET amount = 40.00 WHERE id = 102;

title: Price Hike
story: Supplier costs went up. Increase the price by 10% for ALL products in the Electronics category. Remember that modifying a column based on its own value works perfectly in SQL.
pipelineCallout: Notice how UPDATE operates on sets. The WHERE clause acts exactly like Step 2 of the pipeline: it filters rows down to Electronics, then applies the change to all of them.
hints: Set the price to price * 1.1
Filter the category using WHERE.
UPDATE Products SET price = price * 1.1 WHERE category = 'Electronics';

title: Remove Cancelled Orders
story: It's time for some database cleanup. DELETE all orders that have a status of 'Cancelled'.
hints: Use DELETE FROM table WHERE condition.
DELETE FROM Orders WHERE status = 'Cancelled';

title: Selective Cleanup
story: Data integrity check: sometimes products get deleted, leaving "orphan" orders. DELETE all orders whose product_id is NOT IN the current Products table. This previews Level 10's subquery logic.
hints: You can use a subquery inside your WHERE clause: WHERE product_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM Products)
DELETE FROM Orders WHERE product_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM Products);

title: Email Prefix
story: We want to hash parts of emails for security. To start, extract just the first 4 characters of each customer's email. Return first_name and use SUBSTR(). Exclude NULL emails.
pipelineCallout: Functions work within an existing pipeline step. When you use SUBSTR in SELECT, it applies to each row as it passes through Step 5.
hints: SUBSTR(string_column, start_position, length)
In SQLite, SUBSTR uses a 1-based index, so start at 1.
SELECT first_name, SUBSTR(email, 1, 4) AS email_prefix FROM Customers WHERE email IS NOT NULL;

title: Find the @ Symbol
story: To split an email address, we first need to know where the "@" character is. Use INSTR() to find the exact position number of the "@" symbol in each email. Again, exclude NULLs.
hints: INSTR(string_column, '@') returns the position index.
SELECT first_name, INSTR(email, '@') AS at_position FROM Customers WHERE email IS NOT NULL;

title: Email Username
story: Now combine the previous two concepts! Extract just the username (everything before the "@" symbol) for each customer using SUBSTR() combined with INSTR().
hints: The length of the substring you want is the position of the '@' minus 1.
Use INSTR inside your SUBSTR call.
SELECT first_name, SUBSTR(email, 1, INSTR(email, '@') - 1) AS username FROM Customers WHERE email IS NOT NULL;

title: Customer Email Domain
story: Extract the domain (everything after the "@"). If you omit the third argument of SUBSTR, it grabs everything to the end of the string.
hints: Start the substring at the index of the '@' plus 1.
SELECT first_name, SUBSTR(email, INSTR(email, '@') + 1) AS domain FROM Customers WHERE email IS NOT NULL;

title: Uppercase Roster
story: Formatting requirement for name badges: return employee last_name entirely in uppercase, and first_name entirely in lowercase. Use UPPER() and LOWER().
hints: Just wrap the columns in the respective functions.
SELECT UPPER(last_name) AS upper_last, LOWER(first_name) AS lower_first FROM Employees;

title: Name Length Ranking
story: Rank employees by the length of their full name (first + space + last). Use LENGTH() and ORDER BY. Return the concatenated full name and its length.
hints: Remember to generate the full name using || to measure its length correctly.
SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name, LENGTH(first_name || ' ' || last_name) AS name_length FROM Employees ORDER BY name_length DESC, full_name ASC;

title: Safe Email Display
story: For a public forum, we need to hide actual email formats. Take the email column and use REPLACE() to swap out the "@" symbol with a safe " [at] ".
hints: REPLACE(column_name, search_string, replacement_string)
SELECT first_name, REPLACE(email, '@', ' [at] ') AS safe_email FROM Customers WHERE email IS NOT NULL;

title: Integer Price Tags
story: Marketing is doing a "Round Dollar" promotion. List name and price from Products, then use CAST() to turn the decimal price into an INTEGER, dropping the cents.
hints: CAST(column_name AS type)
SELECT name, price, CAST(price AS INTEGER) AS price_int FROM Products ORDER BY price DESC;

title: Days Since Registration
story: How old is each customer account relative to our reporting date? In SQLite, julianday() converts dates to fractional days. Subtract the registration date from '2025-01-01', and CAST to INTEGER.
hints: julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(registration_date)
CAST the final result as an INTEGER to get whole days.
SELECT first_name, CAST(julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(registration_date) AS INTEGER) AS days_since FROM Customers;

title: Tenure in Years
story: This is deliberately hard. Calculate employee tenure in whole years relative to '2025-01-01'. Get the difference in days using julianday(), divide by 365.25 for leap years, and CAST to INTEGER. Return first, last, and years.
hints: Follow the math logic step by step using parentheses.
CAST( (julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 AS INTEGER)
SELECT first_name, last_name, CAST((julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 AS INTEGER) AS tenure_years FROM Employees;

title: Price Tiers
story: Marketing needs to segment products by price. Return name, price, and a derived column called price_tier. Use CASE WHEN: if price < 50, "Budget". If price <= 500, "Mid-range". Else "Premium".
pipelineCallout: CASE WHEN lives inside the SELECT step. It doesn't filter rows (that's WHERE) or group them (that's GROUP BY). It simply looks at a row when Step 5 is running and computes a new value for it on the fly.
hints: CASE WHEN condition THEN result WHEN condition THEN result ELSE result END
SELECT name, price, CASE WHEN price < 50 THEN 'Budget' WHEN price <= 500 THEN 'Mid-range' ELSE 'Premium' END AS price_tier FROM Products;

title: Order Status Labels
story: The user-facing portal needs friendlier labels. Return id, status, and a display_status using CASE: Processing → "In Progress", Shipped → "On Its Way", Delivered → "Completed", Cancelled → "Cancelled". For anything else, return the raw status.
hints: You can chain multiple WHEN clauses inside your CASE statement.

SELECT id, status, CASE WHEN status = 'Processing' THEN 'In Progress' WHEN status = 'Shipped' THEN 'On Its Way' WHEN status = 'Delivered' THEN 'Completed' WHEN status = 'Cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled' ELSE status END AS display_status FROM Orders;

title: Salary Bands
story: HR wants a headcount by experience level. Count employees in each salary band. Use CASE to create the band: < 50000 → "Junior", <= 80000 → "Mid", Else → "Senior". GROUP BY this new band. Show band and headcount.
hints: You can either GROUP BY the CASE expression itself, or rely on the column alias depending on the SQL engine.
SELECT CASE WHEN salary < 50000 THEN 'Junior' WHEN salary <= 80000 THEN 'Mid' ELSE 'Senior' END AS band, COUNT(*) AS headcount FROM Employees GROUP BY band;

title: Company Directory
story: We want a unified list of everyone in our system. Extract first_name as name from Customers, and attach a static string 'Customer' as type. Do the same for Employees with 'Employee'. Combine both queries using UNION. Sort alphabetically by name.
pipelineCallout: UNION runs the full pipeline twice — once for the top query, once for the bottom query — then stacks the two results together, finally applying the bottom ORDER BY to the combined stack.
hints: Query 1 UNION Query 2 ORDER BY ...
SELECT first_name AS name, 'Customer' AS type FROM Customers UNION SELECT first_name AS name, 'Employee' AS type FROM Employees ORDER BY name;

title: All People, All Sources
story: Do the exact same thing as the previous query, but use UNION ALL. Notice if any rows change. UNION removes duplicates across the stacked tables; UNION ALL keeps every single row, even if they match perfectly.
hints: Simply replace UNION with UNION ALL from your last answer.
SELECT first_name AS name, 'Customer' AS type FROM Customers UNION ALL SELECT first_name AS name, 'Employee' AS type FROM Employees ORDER BY name;

title: Conditional Revenue Flag
story: Finance wants a split metric from the Orders table: what's our total pipeline value for High Value (>200) vs Low Value (<=200) orders? Return two columns, `high_revenue` and `low_revenue`, by wrapping a CASE WHEN statement inside a SUM().
hints: SUM(CASE WHEN condition THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS metric_name
SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN amount > 200 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS high_revenue, SUM(CASE WHEN amount <= 200 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS low_revenue FROM Orders;

title: Above-Average Earners
story: Welcome to the capstone level. Let's start with a scalar subquery. Return first_name and salary for employees earning more than the company's average salary. Sort by salary descending.
pipelineCallout: A subquery inside WHERE executes while Step 2 is running. It evaluates its own mini-pipeline to produce a single value, then passes that value back to the main WHERE filter.
hints: Put another SELECT inside parentheses within your WHERE clause.
WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM Employees)
SELECT first_name, salary FROM Employees WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM Employees) ORDER BY salary DESC;

title: Most Expensive Product Buyer
story: Who ordered the most expensive item? Find the first_name and last_name from Customers (using DISTINCT) using a subquery inside the WHERE clause based on the `Orders` and `Products` tables.
hints: WHERE product_id = (SELECT id FROM Products WHERE price = (SELECT MAX(price) FROM Products))
SELECT DISTINCT c.first_name, c.last_name FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.product_id = (SELECT id FROM Products WHERE price = (SELECT MAX(price) FROM Products));

title: Customer Order Summary
story: Let's clean things up using a CTE (Common Table Expression). Calculate the total spent per customer_id in a CTE named cs. Then write a main query that simply selects all columns from cs.
pipelineCallout: A CTE runs completely first, acting as Step 0. The database executes it, stores it in memory as a virtual table, and then your main query's FROM step just reads from it like a normal table.
hints: Use WITH cte_name AS (query) SELECT ...
WITH cs AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT customer_id, total_spent FROM cs;

title: Rank Products by Price
story: Window functions! Rank all products from most to least expensive. Return name, price, and RANK() OVER (ORDER BY price DESC) as price_rank.
pipelineCallout: Step 4½ is unlocked. Window functions execute after GROUP BY and HAVING, but right before the final SELECT projection. They can see the whole dataset but don't collapse it into single rows.
hints: Follow the exact syntax in the prompt: RANK() OVER (ORDER BY price DESC)
SELECT name, price, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY price DESC) AS price_rank FROM Products;

title: Employee Salary Ranking
story: Rank employees by salary, highest first. Notice how Bob and Fiona, who both earn 85000, get the tied rank of 2. The next person gets rank 4. That's how RANK() works.
hints: Apply RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC)
SELECT first_name, salary, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank FROM Employees;

title: Department Salary Leaderboard
story: Rank employees by salary within each department. Return `first_name`, `department`, `salary`, and rank. Use PARTITION BY department inside the OVER clause.
hints: RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC)
SELECT first_name, department, salary, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank FROM Employees;

title: Order Row Numbers
story: Number each order chronologically using ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY order_date). Unlike RANK(), ROW_NUMBER() guarantees unique numbers even if dates tie.
hints: SELECT id, order_date, amount, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS row_num FROM Orders;

title: Running Revenue
story: Compute a cumulative running total of revenue over time. Use SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) as running_total. This is the magic of window functions.
hints: SELECT order_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS running_total FROM Orders;

title: Pipeline Check: CTE + Window
story: This is not a coding problem. Arrange the execution order for a complex query involving a CTE and a Window Function. This tests your understanding of Step 4½.
query:
WITH customer_spending AS (
    SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT c.first_name, cs.total_spent,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY cs.total_spent DESC) AS spending_rank
FROM Customers c
JOIN customer_spending cs ON c.id = cs.customer_id
WHERE cs.total_spent > 100
ORDER BY spending_rank
LIMIT 5
debrief: The CTE runs its own mini-pipeline first. RANK() runs at Step 4½ — after WHERE filtered out customers below 100, but before SELECT. That's why `spending_rank` starts at 1 even though some customers were excluded.

title: Top Customers by Spending
story: The grand combination. Create a CTE that sums order amounts per customer. Then JOIN that CTE to the Customers table to rank them by total spending. Return first_name, total_spent, and spending_rank.
hints: WITH cs AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT c.first_name, cs.total_spent, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY cs.total_spent DESC) AS spending_rank FROM cs JOIN Customers c ON cs.customer_id = c.id ORDER BY spending_rank;

title: Rank vs Dense Rank
story: Apply both RANK() and DENSE_RANK() to employee salaries. See the difference? Bob and Fiona tie at 85k (rank 2). RANK gives the next person rank 4. DENSE_RANK gives them rank 3. There are no gaps in DENSE_RANK.
hints: SELECT first_name, salary, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dense_rank FROM Employees;

title: Pipeline Check: Design Your Own
story: Final Problem. Write a query from scratch that uses at least 5 of the 8 pipeline steps (FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, Window, SELECT, ORDER BY, LIMIT). There is no exact expected output here. Test your query, make sure it runs, and verify it uses 5 steps. When you're confident, hit Run Query to complete badcode.
pipelineCallout: You've reached the end. The training wheels are off. You now know exactly how a database executes your instructions beneath the surface.
hints: A full query might look like: SELECT category, AVG(price) FROM Products WHERE stock > 0 GROUP BY category HAVING count(*) > 0 ORDER BY AVG(price) LIMIT 10;
Any valid query that runs without error will pass this open challenge.
